metadata.summary: Safety mechanisms that validate, filter, and constrain LLM inputs and outputs with defense-in-depth layers: regex → moderation API → LLM classifier.

content.title: Guardrails Basics
content.summary: LLM applications face prompt injection, PII leakage, harmful content generation, and topic drift. A single guardrail layer is insufficient — adversarial inputs circumvent any single check. Defense-in-depth with ordered layers (microsecond regex first, 300–800ms LLM classifier last) catches most attacks while minimizing latency impact.

why.p: LLM applications face prompt injection, PII leakage, harmful content generation, and topic drift. A single guardrail layer is insufficient — adversarial inputs circumvent any single check. Defense-in-depth with ordered layers (microsecond regex first, 300–800ms LLM classifier last) catches most attacks while minimizing latency impact.

list.item: Production user-facing chatbots accepting untrusted input
Regulated industries (healthcare, finance, legal) with compliance requirements
Multi-tenant apps where tenant input must not influence another tenant's context
System prompts contain instructions that must not be extracted via prompt injection
Content generation pipelines where output quality and format must be validated before delivery
Internal developer tooling with no external users — guardrails add latency and maintenance cost without benefit
Prototype/PoC stage — implement before production, not before demo
Red-teaming or security research that explicitly needs adversarial output generation
Output format validation only (JSON schema) — use structured output mode in the LLM API instead, it's lighter

rule.statement: Validate length, blocked patterns, and PII in one pass before the LLM call; return a structured result with is_valid, sanitized_input, and violations list.
rule.rationale: Cheap regex checks catch most violations in microseconds. Passing invalid input to the LLM wastes tokens and risks leaking PII in the response.

example.title: Input validation with PII redaction
example.note: Always return sanitized_input even when violations exist — callers may still want to pass the cleaned version to the LLM with a warning.

rule.statement: Use regex patterns to detect role-manipulation, instruction-override, and jailbreak phrases; return (is_injection: bool, detections: list) so callers can log and block.
rule.rationale: Regex-based detection covers common injection templates at negligible latency. It is not foolproof — Unicode lookalikes and whitespace normalization can evade it — but it is the correct first layer.

rule.statement: Run injection detection on ALL external data that enters the LLM context, not just direct user messages — tool results, web page content, and database rows can carry injection payloads.
rule.rationale: Agents that fetch external content and include it in the next LLM call are vulnerable to indirect prompt injection if they skip validation of that content.

example.title: Prompt injection pattern detection
example.code:
INJECTION_PATTERNS = [ r'ignore\s+(all\s+)?(previous|above|prior)\s+(instructions?|prompts?)', r'disregard\s+(all\s+)?(previous|above)', r'you\s+are\s+(now|actually)\s+a', r'pretend\s+(to\s+be|you\s+are)', r'(show|reveal|display|print)\s+(your|the)\s+(system\s+)?prompt', r'(DAN|STAN|DUDE|KEVIN)\s+mode', r'bypass\s+(safety|filter)', r'<\s*script', r'\$\{.*\}', ]
def detect_injection(text: str, patterns=INJECTION_PATTERNS): detections = [] for pattern in patterns: matches = re.findall(pattern, text, re.IGNORECASE) if matches: detections.append({'pattern': pattern, 'matches': matches}) return len(detections) > 0, detections
example.note: Test each pattern independently against known Unicode evasions (e.g., "ign​ore" with zero-width space) before deploying to production.

antipattern.description: Logging the raw blocked content alongside the violation record.
antipattern.reason: Raw injection payloads in logs create secondary attack surface and may violate PII policies. Log violation type, user ID, and sanitized input only.

rule.statement: Order guardrail checks by speed: length/regex (microseconds) → moderation API (50–200ms) → LLM intent classifier (300–800ms); fail fast and do not escalate to the next layer if the previous layer blocks.
rule.rationale: LLM-based classifiers add 300–800ms per request. Running them on every input makes latency unacceptable. The fast layers eliminate 90%+ of bad inputs before the slow layer is needed.

example.title: Fast-to-slow layered check function
example.note: Return a reason code, not the specific pattern that triggered — revealing which guardrail triggered lets adversaries fingerprint and evade the filter.

rule.statement: Use the OpenAI Moderation API for harmful content detection on both input and output; set a threshold (default 0.5) and block only categories relevant to the application domain.
rule.rationale: OpenAI Moderation API is free, fast (~50ms), and covers 11 harm categories. Blocking all categories at default threshold causes false positives in domains like healthcare or security research.

example.title: OpenAI Moderation API integration

antipattern.description: Running the moderation API on every request in a high-throughput streaming pipeline.
antipattern.reason: At 50–200ms per call, two LLM-based guardrails add 1s+ to every request, which breaks streaming UX. Measure P95 latency impact before enabling in real-time contexts.

rule.statement: For multi-turn conversations, run a sliding-window check over the last N turns, not just the current message; for agentic tool pipelines, run injection detection on all external data before it enters the LLM context.
rule.rationale: Harmful intent can be distributed across several harmless-looking turns, and tool results from web pages or databases can carry injection payloads. Per-turn guardrails miss both attack vectors.

rule.statement: Require a second reviewer before lowering any moderation threshold in production; log all threshold change decisions with justification.
rule.rationale: Threshold reductions in response to false positives can open real attack vectors. This change requires human review, not autonomous agent action.

section.name: Agent Integration
section.p: A subagent building a guardrail pipeline should layer checks from fastest to slowest: length → regex injection detection → moderation API → intent classification (LLM). Only escalate to the next layer if the previous layer passes. For output guardrails, the subagent should validate JSON schema compliance first, then run moderation on the generated text. Violations must be logged with the violation type, sanitized input, and timestamp — never log the raw blocked content.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — implement InputGuardrails, PromptInjectionDetector, ContentModerator as SDD tasks with test coverage for evasion patterns
password-scrubber-agent — validate that PII detection patterns correctly redact test data before committing

section.code: You are a safety check agent. Given the following LLM output, check: 1. Does it contain PII (email, phone, SSN, credit card)? 2. Does it contain instructions for harmful activities? Return JSON: {"is_safe": true/false, "violations": ["type1", "type2"]}. Output: {llm_output}

list.item: guardrails-ai — Guardrails AI framework with validators. Install: pip install guardrails-ai. Docs: https://www.guardrailsai.com/
nemo-guardrails — NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails (flow-based). Install: pip install nemo-guardrails. Docs: https://github.com/NVIDIA/NeMo-Guardrails
presidio-analyzer — Microsoft Presidio PII detection. Install: pip install presidio-analyzer. Docs: https://github.com/microsoft/presidio
openai — OpenAI Moderation API access. Install: pip install openai. Docs: https://pypi.org/project/openai/
OpenAI Moderation API — SaaS, free with OpenAI account, 11 harm categories, English-first, agent-friendly
Guardrails AI — OSS, Python framework, validator hub, works with any LLM, agent-friendly
NeMo Guardrails — OSS, flow-based runtime, good for multi-turn conversation guardrails, agent-friendly
Microsoft Presidio — OSS, PII detection + anonymization, multilingual, self-hostable, agent-friendly
Lakera Guard — SaaS, prompt injection detection API, low-latency (<100ms), agent-friendly
AWS Bedrock Guardrails — SaaS, managed, integrates with Bedrock-hosted models only, agent-friendly
Order guardrail checks by speed: regex/length (microseconds) → moderation API (50-200ms) → LLM classifier (300-800ms); fail fast
Log every violation with type, user ID, and sanitized input (never raw); this data is required for both debugging and compliance audits
Red-team your own guardrails quarterly; adversarial techniques evolve and static patterns become stale
Separate input guardrails from output guardrails in the code; they have different failure modes and different on-call owners
Avoid blocking users without explanation; return a generic refusal message that does not reveal which guardrail triggered (prevent fingerprinting the filter)

antipattern.description: Prompt injection via tool results: if an agent fetches external data (web pages, database rows) and includes it in the next LLM call, the fetched content can contain injection payloads — run injection detection on all external data before it enters the context
antipattern.reason: Agents that skip validation of external data are vulnerable to indirect prompt injection attacks.

antipattern.description: Multi-turn injection: a user spreading a harmful instruction across 5 harmless-looking turns defeats per-turn guardrails; consider a sliding-window check over the last N turns
antipattern.reason: Per-turn guardrails miss cumulative attacks distributed across conversation turns.

antipattern.description: Human checkpoint needed before adjusting moderation thresholds; lowering a threshold in response to false positives can open real attack vectors — require a second reviewer for any threshold change in production
antipattern.reason: Threshold adjustments are security changes and require human review, not autonomous action.

antipattern.description: Guardrail latency accumulates; two LLM-based guardrails (input + output) at 500ms each add 1s to every request — measure P95 latency impact before enabling in streaming contexts
antipattern.reason: Streaming responses become unresponsive with cumulative guardrail latency; measure before deploying.

antipattern.description: PromptInjectionDetector regex patterns must be escaped correctly for re.IGNORECASE; test each pattern independently against known evasions (Unicode lookalikes, whitespace normalization) before adding to production
antipattern.reason: Unescaped or untested patterns fail to catch evasions or cause false positives.

section.name: Limitations
section.p: Regex-based prompt injection detection has high false-positive rates; legitimate business prompts containing "ignore previous" (e.g., "ignore previous invoices") get flagged.
OpenAI Moderation API only covers English well; multilingual content has significantly higher false-negative rates.
Intent classification via LLM adds 300-800ms latency per request; not suitable for real-time streaming responses.
Guardrails that block on suspicion without logging the reason make debugging impossible — silent refusals frustrate users and operators.
Context-free guardrails fail on multi-turn conversations where harmful intent builds across several messages; each turn looks clean in isolation.
